linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.title: Linux Terminal Fundamentals
linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.description: Learn the Linux terminal from the ground up through safe inspection, navigation, file organization, and cumulative terminal-workspace projects.

linux-module-1-terminal-navigation.title: Terminal Orientation and Navigation
linux-module-1-terminal-navigation.description: Begin with the terminal as a readable conversation, then learn to confirm your location and move through folders without guessing.
outcomes: Explain command, prompt, and output in simple terms.
Use `pwd` and `ls` to inspect a workspace.
Move with `cd`, `cd ..`, and simple relative paths.
Complete a navigation checkpoint without changing files.
why: Terminal work becomes safer when you can always answer two questions: where am I, and what is here?
Navigation is the foundation for every later file command in this course.

linux-module-2-files-and-folders.title: Build, Write, and Inspect Files
linux-module-2-files-and-folders.description: Turn navigation into practical file work: create structures, write text, inspect content, copy safely, move deliberately, rename clearly, and remove only known temporary files.
outcomes: Create folders and placeholder files with `mkdir`, `mkdir -p`, and `touch`.
Write and append text with `echo`, `>`, and `>>`.
Use `cp`, `mv`, and safe `rm` in a realistic handoff workflow.
why: Most terminal work is not about memorizing commands; it is about changing a workspace carefully and checking the result.
These commands form a repeatable workflow you can use in coding projects, school folders, and everyday file organization.

linux-module-3-final-capstone.title: Final Capstone: Community Media Archive
linux-module-3-final-capstone.description: Transfer the complete beginner command set into a new media-archive scenario and finish with an explicit verification pass.
outcomes: Create a clean folder structure for a real handoff.
Write, move, copy, inspect, and safely remove files.
Leave a ready marker and README for the next person.
why: A capstone should prove that you can choose and combine commands without being led one command at a time.
The final verification pass turns a collection of file operations into a dependable handoff.

linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.title: Terminal Orientation
linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.description: Start with the safest commands: inspect where you are, see what is nearby, and move between folders.

linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.title: Navigation Checkpoint
linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.description: Use navigation commands in a small folder-tour story without changing files.

linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.title: File Workflow
linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.description: Create, write, inspect, copy, move, rename, and safely remove files.

linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.title: Class Notes Handoff
linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.description: Build a notes folder, write a README, move notes, and remove scratch safely.

linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.title: Final Capstone
linux--linux-terminal-fundamentals--draf.description: Clean a community file room with the full beginner terminal toolkit.
